review_summary: The transcript clearly supports two introductory foundation-model questions, so no separate clip is necessary.

question_bank.question: Which option best captures the main difference between a traditional task-specific model and a foundation model?
choices: A task-specific model is trained for one task, while a foundation model is broadly pretrained and then adapted to many tasks
A task-specific model always uses self-supervision, while a foundation model always uses labels
A task-specific model cannot use neural networks, while a foundation model must use transformers
A task-specific model predicts only text, while a foundation model predicts only images
question_bank.explanation: The central shift is from building one model per task to broadly pretraining a single model that can later be adapted across many downstream tasks.

question_bank.question: Which trait is most characteristic of foundation models?
choices: They are usually trained at large scale and aim to be robust across many tasks
They always require a separate labeled dataset for every new use case
They can only be used for language and not for vision
They avoid self-supervised objectives in favor of hand-coded rules
question_bank.explanation: Foundation models are typically designed to generalize broadly, and that usually goes together with large models, large datasets, and self-supervised or weakly supervised pretraining.
